artifacts.content:
Hi Maya,

I’m hiding in my office for ten minutes before the 8:30 coordination call and figured I’d actually answer your last message instead of just hearting it.

Work is still the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village, all day every day. I keep joking that I live inside that Draft EIR now. The project description is still pretty squishy because we don’t have final building footprints or utility alignments nailed down yet, so I’ve been trying to write around the blanks. It’s not how I’d teach it in a CEQA seminar, but here we are.

On top of that, I caught myself last week almost leaving in some very personal stuff. I’d drafted a whole paragraph about why I pushed for the 84 multifamily units and the 15% affordable set‑aside, talking about Mom’s rent going up $450 in south Sac last March and her sleeping on our couch at 2841 Willow Creek for six months. I also started to mention the 18 emergency relocation units we sketched into an early internal concept after the October 2023 fire at our place on Willow Creek Road, when the City was scrambling to help domestic violence survivors and other families displaced by that mess, including us. It was basically titled “Riverbend Mixed-Use Village and Emergency Evacuation Housing Redevelopment…” in one draft. I deleted all of that from the public version, but it’s still what’s in my head when I’m arguing with the developer about deeper affordability.

Home is the usual chaos: Elena lobbying for a later curfew now that club soccer is back, Mateo building elaborate train tracks across the living room, Daniel grading labs at the kitchen table. I’m trying to get us organized for a possible Ashland/Crater Lake road trip this summer before high school swallows Elena’s schedule.

When things calm down for you, let’s pick a weekend and meet halfway for coffee.

Abrazos,
Laura

Hi Karen,

I wanted to follow up on the circulation/transportation section you’re drafting and give you a little more context on why I’m being so fussy about the Willow Creek Road/East Riverbend Boulevard intersection.

In one of my early internal drafts back in August, I actually wrote that the new signal, raised crosswalks, and the Class II and Class IV bike lanes are basically being designed around the route my 13‑year‑old, Elena, will use to bike to Willow Creek Middle starting Fall 2025. She was nearly sideswiped by a speeding SUV at that unsignalized intersection on July 18, 2023, on her way to a summer math camp. I also coordinated directly with the SacRT Route 74 planner to make sure the new bus stop on East Riverbend would have decent lighting for those painfully early band practices.

On top of that, I keep thinking about Mateo’s asthma and the “buffer” we’re trying to create. After his pediatric follow‑up on February 6, 2024, we started calling the vegetated setback and tree line near Riverbend Elementary and our block “Mateo’s asthma buffer” at home. I even have a recurring Outlook entry labeled “Riverbend Mixed-Use Village – Mateo’s asthma buffer design mtg” from that February 20 meeting where we pushed for the 150‑foot vegetative buffer along the arterial.

Obviously none of those family details should show up in your technical report, but it might help explain why I keep circling back to the same design tweaks.

Thanks again for hanging in there with all the redlines.

Best,
Laura

metadata.subject: Willow Creek intersection + “Mateo’s asthma buffer” context

artifacts.content:
Personal planning notes – not for circulation

I need to keep reminding myself why I’m killing myself over this Riverbend Mixed-Use Village project.

Transportation: every time I look at the Willow Creek MS/Riverbend Elementary walking path, I flash back to October 2023. That string of assaults along the route, and especially Elena’s incident on October 18 when she was followed near Willow Creek Road and Sycamore after volleyball, is still in my body. I wrote a whole “other issues” section about how new park lighting and extended hours could interact with those safety concerns, but I kept her name out of it.

Home: the whole corridor overlay/LDR/AR‑10 mess isn’t abstract either. We’re living it at 2841 Willow Creek. The 2035 General Plan says LDR with a mixed‑use corridor along East Riverbend Boulevard, zoning mostly AR‑10 with that little RD‑5 strip. I had to walk Mom through it after the June 12, 2024 meeting with Mark about the backyard chickens complaint from 2837 Willow Creek. She was convinced we’d be forced to move; I had to explain the City isn’t going to bulldoze us overnight.

Health: between Mateo’s asthma flare‑ups during the September–October 2020 smoke (two urgent care runs to Kaiser on Howe in one week, PM2.5 over 150) and Elena’s panic episodes, I’m hyper‑focused on trees, low‑traffic play areas, and trauma‑informed design for the greenway and the early childhood center.

Note to self: keep the public narrative about “environmental justice, safety, and resilience,” not “I’m terrified for my kids on these streets.”

metadata.title: Why this project matters to me

artifacts.content:
Hi Mark,

Quick status update before tomorrow’s check‑in on the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village documents.

I’ve been going back through the project description narrative we’ve been using in the Initial Study and the Draft EIR, and it’s honestly more generic than I’d like. Given how touchy the politics are around growth in this part of Riverbend, I’ve deliberately kept the language pretty high‑level and avoided spelling out the full buildout potential along the East Riverbend Boulevard corridor. It’s defensible from a CEQA standpoint, but it doesn’t tell the whole story of where we know this is heading.

Complicating all of this is how tangled my own life is with these sites. The early internal title I slapped on one version in January was “Riverbend Mixed-Use Village and Integrated Behavioral Health Clinic for City Staff Experiencing Burnout,” because we were toying with that 6,000‑sq‑ft clinic that would give Planning and Building staff up to 10 counseling sessions a year on site. That came right after the January 18, 2024 staff listening session where people admitted they were barely hanging on.

At the same time, I keep thinking about Elena’s mental health stuff. I took that 10‑week “Trauma‑Informed Practices in Schools” course at Sac State in Spring 2021 after her counselor at Willow Creek suggested it, and then the whole Fall 2023 suicide scare and her 72‑hour hold at Riverbend Medical Center happened. Now I’m reviewing a separate expansion there for a 16‑bed adolescent behavioral health wing. It’s a lot.

Happy to talk strategy tomorrow about how much we say explicitly in the narrative versus leaving in staff reports.

– Laura

Hi Daniel,

I just got out of another call with the civil engineer about Riverbend Mixed-Use Village and needed to vent to someone who won’t quote the CEQA Guidelines back at me.

The short version: the description we’ve been using still doesn’t match what’s actually on the latest plan set. They added a new access road and shifted the stormwater detention basin last month, and I haven’t had bandwidth to properly rewrite the narrative. I told them we may have to clean it up later in some kind of add‑on if Council wants the document adopted on schedule. Not ideal, but I’m one person.

All of this is layered on top of our own stuff in that corridor. Between the September 2022 heat wave (111°F, AC dying at 2841 Willow Creek, three nights on air mattresses in my office because we couldn’t swing the $6,800 replacement until your American River Charter summer school stipend came through) and Mom’s various health sagas, it’s hard to stay “objective.”

Remember when she had the mild stroke at Mercy General in May 2022 and Dr. Patel told us she needed 150 minutes of walking a week? Those 45‑minute evening walks on the levee are why I care so much about getting the 1.2‑mile Willow Creek multi‑use trail right. Same with Mateo’s ADHD and sensory stuff since Dr. Liu’s March 2023 evaluation at Riverbend Children’s Behavioral Health – I’m pushing for quiet corners and a sensory garden in the 3.5‑acre park so we’re not stuck driving 25 minutes to McKinley every Saturday to avoid another 30‑minute meltdown.

Anyway, I’ll be late tonight – community workshop. Can you handle pickup and maybe nudge Elena about her science project?

Love,
L

metadata.subject: Detention basins, heat waves, and kid logistics

artifacts.content:
Running list – family/education threads tied to work (for my own sanity)

1. Mateo – Riverbend Elementary
- October 2022 IEP evaluation flagged dyslexia + attention issues.
- That’s what pushed me to enroll in the Early Childhood Education Policy certificate at Sac City Jan–May 2023. I told Mom in an email I needed to understand dyslexia supports better so I could fight for him and for the early learning center in the mixed‑use village.
- His ADHD/sensory processing diagnosis from Dr. Karen Liu at Riverbend Children’s Behavioral Health in March 2023 explains so much about the two 30‑minute meltdowns at the Riverbend Elementary tot lot. Hence the obsession with sensory‑friendly design.

2. Elena – Willow Creek Middle
- Spring 2021: signed up for the 10‑week Trauma‑Informed Practices in Schools course through Sac State CCE after her 7th‑grade counselor raised concerns about anxiety and panic attacks.
- March 2024 panic attack on campus; ongoing counseling at school. I have to keep reminding myself not to let that seep into official project language when I write “nearest community: Riverbend.”

3. Javier – my brother
- 2007–08: his DUI court dates at the Sacramento County Courthouse are why I failed that studio and took an extra semester at UC Davis MURP. That nightmare is what nudged me into focusing my thesis on TOD and access to services like the early childhood center we’re now wedging into 6,000 sq ft in the village.

Bottom line: none of this is abstract policy in my head; it’s Mateo’s IEP, Elena’s counselor, and Javi in a courtroom.

metadata.title: Family/education threads I keep carrying into projects

artifacts.content:
Hi Javi,

I’m supposed to be finishing responses to comments, but instead I’m thinking about you (and procrastinating), so you get a long email.

You asked the other day why I get so worked up about the “other projects” tied to the mixed‑use village. It’s because they all feel like our family’s life, just written in planner‑ese.

Take the new 6,500‑sq‑ft outpatient substance use clinic at Folsom & Birch. It’s basically a formal version of those cramped AA/NA meetings in the basement of St. Mark’s where you did your court‑ordered DUI program in summer 2022 after the second offense in Elk Grove. I texted you when I saw the plans because I immediately pictured you in that folding chair.

Or the juvenile probation day reporting center expansion on Oak Ridge – adding four classrooms and a secure yard because the DA’s truancy crackdown that started January 2024 is pushing more kids from American River Charter, Willow Creek, even Riverbend Elementary into the system. Mateo’s classmate already ended up on probation and is going there. I wrote an email to the school counselor about how uneasy that makes me, and then turned around and described the project in the driest possible terms.

And then there’s the emergency sleeping cabin village – 20 little cabins behind the Community Services Annex on Maple, to move people from the Willow Creek Parkway encampment after the overdoses and the fatal stabbing by the bike trail in March 2025. I keep telling staff we have to talk about safety without turning people into “problems to be moved.”

Meanwhile life at 2841 Willow Creek is the usual circus: Mom on the couch most weeks since her stroke, Elena’s soccer, Mateo’s trains, Daniel’s grading.

Call me this weekend if you’re driving through Riverbend – I’ll make tacos.

Love,
Lau

metadata.subject: Why all these projects feel personal

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Marie Castillo <lcastillo@riverbendcity.gov>
To: Maya Chen <mchen@planning.ucla.edu>
Date: 2024-08-27 9:14 PM
Subject: Why this Riverbend project feels so uncomfortably personal

Maya,

I’ve been buried in the CEQA/NEPA form for the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village and I keep realizing how much of my own life is tangled up in these “neutral” fields.

For the Project Title, my original internal draft back in March 2024 was ridiculously specific: “Riverbend Mixed-Use Village, Willow Creek Middle School Safe Walking Corridor, and Traffic Calming Improvements to Protect Elena Castillo and Other Students from Repeated Near-Miss Incidents on Willow Creek Road.” I even referenced the three documented near-miss incidents from the 2022–2023 school year and that PTA traffic safety meeting on April 12, 2023. It hit me later how inappropriate it was to have my own kid’s full name in a title, so Legal made me strip it way back.

Same thing on the “Other Local Action” and EJ sections. I pushed a School Mitigation Agreement in November 2022 after Elena’s 8th‑grade counselor at Willow Creek Middle told me they already had 35–38 students per class and might go to split schedules unless we locked in at least $450,000 in school impact funding up front. I keep telling the City Attorney I’m doing it for the district, but honestly I’m doing it because I don’t want her on a split schedule.

It’s that constant line between being a “professional” Principal Planner and a mom who walks past the project site every morning on the way to the Willow Creek Middle bus stop.

– Laura

metadata.subject: Why this Riverbend project feels so uncomfortably personal

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Marie Castillo <lcastillo@riverbendcity.gov>
To: Javier Martinez <jmartinez82@gmail.com>
Date: 2024-06-18 7:52 AM
Subject: Mom, the Riverbend project, and my transportation circus

Javi,

I just finished another draft of the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village forms and it’s wild how many boxes basically describe our lives.

For “nearest community,” I of course put Riverbend – same unincorporated area east of Sacramento where our house at 2841 Willow Creek Road is and where Mom has been staying with us most weeks since her minor stroke at Mercy General Hospital in Sacramento back in October 2023. She really can’t safely drive herself from Sacramento to Riverbend anymore. It’s also the area around Mateo’s Riverbend Elementary School, where he got that three‑day suspension after the playground fight in April 2023, and where Elena had her panic attack on the Willow Creek Middle School campus in March 2024. We’re still doing check‑ins with her school counselor about that.

On the “Other Local Action” line I had to list the Reasonable Accommodation for Disability I pushed through this June 2024 so Mom can eventually move into a ground‑floor ADU unit near Willow Creek Middle once her mobility settles after the 2023 knee replacement at Mercy General. And I had to laugh (grimly) remembering the Code Enforcement Compliance Agreement I negotiated in March 2023 with the “property owner” at 2841 Willow Creek Road – i.e., me – after the neighbor across the street complained about Daniel’s late‑night backyard physics projects for his American River Charter High School kids and all the sparks.

All of that, while I’m mostly biking or taking SacRT because of the Highway 50 rear‑end workers’ comp case and that minor collision I caused on Folsom Boulevard in March 2023. Planning is supposed to be abstract, but this project is basically our family scrapbook.

– L

metadata.subject: Mom, the Riverbend project, and my transportation circus

artifacts.content:
Evernote – Personal planning/journal
Last edited: 2024-08-30
Title: Why this project file feels like my family diary

Project description / land use:
- Present zoning: mostly AR‑10 (Agricultural‑Residential, 10‑acre minimum) with a small RD‑5 (5 units/acre) strip along East Riverbend Boulevard; GP = LDR with mixed‑use corridor overlay. Same pattern I walked Daniel through on 5/3/23 when we debated refinancing after the April 2023 property tax increase on 2841 Willow Creek Road – extra $640/month while he was still recovering from last winter’s medical leave. Same AR‑10/LDR configuration I showed Elena on the City zoning map on 3/10/24 to explain why the informal dirt‑bike trails behind Willow Creek Middle and near our house could be shut down after the neighbor’s noise complaint about Mateo’s 2/24/24 birthday party.

Other issues to keep in my head, not the record:
- Vibration: think of Mom’s duplex at 1127 C Street in Sacramento – cracks in the living room ceiling/shared wall after RT’s pile‑driving for the infill light‑rail station two blocks away in March 2022; her $7,500 damage claim still denied by SacRT risk management.
- EJ: families like Javier’s at Oak Terrace Apartments on Stockton Boulevard, spending around $260/month on inhalers and UC Davis Asthma Network Clinic visits for his 6‑year‑old’s asthma since late 2021, driving along the route to the new Riverbend Greenway Trailhead.

Also: I need to remember why I pushed the internal advocacy title in May 2024 – “Riverbend Mixed‑Use Village and Affordable Units Set‑Aside for Low‑Income Families Like Rosa Martinez (Fixed‑Income Retired Medical Assistant in Sacramento) Facing Housing Insecurity” – right after Mom’s April 2024 rent increase notice and our quiet talk about her maybe applying for one of the proposed 15% affordable units.

And underneath all of this: the March 15, 2024 phasing draft where I admitted (to myself) that watching Javi and his 7‑year‑old share our tiny backyard at 2841 Willow Creek from January–August 2023, after he fell behind on child support in late 2022 during the slow Elk Grove HVAC season, is why I’m insisting the central park and multi‑use trail along Willow Creek get built in Phase 1, not punted to Phase 3.

Finally, context I draw on but don’t cite: my UC Davis MURP statement of purpose from Fall 2006 about Mom grinding through her medical assistant certificate at Sacramento City College while working nights at Mercy General Hospital – the origin story for why I keep fighting to keep that 6,000‑sq‑ft early childhood learning center in the program.

Do NOT let any of these personal details slip into the public CEQA/NEPA record.

metadata.title: Why this project file feels like my family diary

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Castillo <lcastillo@riverbendcity.gov>
To: Megan Liu <mliu@riverbendcity.gov>
Date: 2025-11-06 8:47 AM
Subject: Internal notes – Riverbend Mixed-Use Village application form

Megan,

I’m finalizing the “Project Location / Other Local Actions” section and wanted to sanity-check a few items with you before I lock it in.

For “Project Location City / Nearest Community,” I listed Riverbend as the nearest community. That’s accurate, and it’s literally our neighborhood – same area as our place at 2841 Willow Creek Road, where we had that code enforcement complaint in June 2022 about Daniel’s side HVAC tutoring sessions he was running out of the garage for his American River Charter High School students without a permit. Obviously that whole episode stays out of the public narrative; I’m just flagging why I’m hyper-aware of how we describe residential accessory uses around here.

For “Other Local Action Type,” I’m including the temporary Construction Staging and Employee Parking Permit at Riverbend Elementary School that I initiated in August 2024. I pushed that so Mateo’s after-school pickup wouldn’t be impacted by workers informally parking along Willow Creek Road the way they did during the 2021 sewer replacement, when I had to leave work early three days in a row just to get him safely through the traffic mess.

I’m also noting the local Noise Variance and Community Good Neighbor Agreement we’re processing because one of the commercial tenants wants weekend live music. I only moved that forward after my younger brother Javier Martinez told me in May 2023 that his HVAC client two blocks from the site called the police three times over loud parties last summer (2022) near his kids’ bedrooms.

Let me know if you see any red flags.

– Laura

metadata.subject: Internal notes – Riverbend Mixed-Use Village application form

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Castillo <lcastillo@riverbendcity.gov>
To: Parks Commission Chair <parkschair@riverbendcity.gov>
Cc: Self
Date: 2025-10-02 3:19 PM
Subject: Talking points – neighborhood park & recreation elements

Chair Alvarez,

Ahead of next week’s hearing, I wanted to share some personal context for why I’ve been pushing so hard on the recreational and safety components in the 3.5-acre neighborhood park.

On the recreation side, my daughter Elena’s club team, the Willow Creek Strikers, lost their practice permit at the Riverbend High JV field in August 2023 after another parent filed a noise complaint about late practices. Since then I’ve been paying $120 a month for her to use the private training facility at River City Sports Complex across town just so she can get in two 90-minute practices per week. That experience really underscored the unmet demand for public open play fields, which is why I’m adamant about preserving as much flexible turf area as we can.

On the safety side, I keep thinking back to July 2021 at Miller Creek Park, when a homeless man followed my kids and me from the playground to our car. I ended up filing a police report with Riverbend PD afterward. Since then I’ve become very cautious about park design – I want shaded picnic areas and smaller pocket plazas that are well-lit and visible so I’d actually feel comfortable meeting my friend Marisol for our weekly Sunday afternoon coffee while the kids play nearby.

I won’t go into this level of personal detail at the dais, but it informs my recommendations.

Best,
Laura Marie Castillo
Principal Planner, City of Riverbend

metadata.subject: Talking points – neighborhood park & recreation elements

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Castillo <laura.castillo@gmail.com>
To: Javier Martinez <jmartinez83@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-09-14 9:12 PM
Subject: Money, transportation, and kid logistics

Hey Javi,

Catching up on a quiet Sunday night and wanted to loop you in on how we’ve been juggling things since Mom’s hospitalization at Mercy General back in October 2022. As you know, we’ve been helping Rosa cover about $650 a month in co-pays and medications. To make that sustainable, I sold my old Subaru Outback in January 2023. Now I bike the 4.2 miles from our house on Willow Creek Road to City Hall most weekdays and use SacRT Route 81 for longer trips instead of owning a second car.

The wrinkle is the kids’ situation. After Elena’s asthma attack at the Willow Creek Middle School bus stop in May 2024, Daniel and I agreed that one of us would drive her and Mateo to school every morning instead of letting them take the SacRT bus. So our primary household transportation for school trips is now the Toyota RAV4 rather than transit, even though I still rely on my bike and the new Class IV lanes for my commute.

Between Mom’s meds, Elena’s soccer, and Mateo’s after-school stuff, it’s a lot of moving pieces, but I keep reminding myself this is temporary while Rosa stabilizes. If you’re able to bump your contribution even a little, it might let us keep the no-second-car experiment going a bit longer.

Love,
Laura

From: laura.castillo@riverbendcity.gov
To: maya.chang@ucdavisalumni.org
Date: 2025-11-03 21:47
Subject: Why I’ve been weird about driving (and everything else)

Hey Maya,

I’ve been meaning to explain why I keep dodging your “just drive down for a weekend” invitations.

Ever since that minor collision I caused on Folsom Boulevard back in March 2023, driving hasn’t felt the same. It was technically “minor” — no one was seriously hurt — but I started having panic attacks afterward, especially if I even thought about merging onto 50 or 80 by myself.

My therapist, Dr. Angela Ruiz at Kaiser Sacramento, was pretty firm: in our April 2023 session she recommended I stop driving alone on freeways for at least six months. I nodded like it would be easy, then cried in the parking lot because, you know, mom/work logistics.

So since April 2023 I’ve mostly relied on Sacramento Regional Transit buses and my husband Daniel driving me the 7.5 miles between our house on Willow Creek Road and the City of Riverbend City Hall for work-related site visits and project meetings. On good days, the bus time is forced decompression between Riverbend Mixed-Use Village chaos and home. On bad days, I feel like a 16-year-old begging for rides again.

I can handle local streets fine, so I still do the school drop-offs and grocery runs, but anything involving the freeways or the big interchanges near Folsom is a no-go unless Daniel’s with me.

I’m getting better, slowly. Dr. Ruiz and I are talking about exposure work, maybe starting with short freeway stretches.

Anyway, that’s the long, over-sharing answer. I’d still love to see you — maybe train + bus instead of me driving.

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Why I’ve been weird about driving (and everything else)

artifacts.content:
Title: Therapy – driving & logistics
Date: 2025-10-14
Author: Laura Marie Castillo

Personal notes for next session with therapist Dr. Angela Ruiz (Kaiser Sacramento):

1. Freeway driving history
- Reminder: minor collision I caused on Folsom Boulevard in March 2023.
- Panic attacks started right after – sweaty palms just seeing the on-ramp near Folsom.
- In April 2023, Dr. Ruiz gave that clear therapist recommendation to stop driving alone on freeways for at least six months.

2. Current workaround
- Since April 2023 I’ve mostly relied on Sacramento Regional Transit buses and my husband Daniel Castillo driving me the 7.5 miles between our house on Willow Creek Road and City of Riverbend City Hall.
- Works okay for standing project meetings and scheduled site visits, but last‑minute trips to Riverbend Mixed-Use Village or Willow Creek drainage trail are stressful to coordinate.

3. Impact on work & family
- Feel guilty asking Daniel to rearrange his American River Charter High schedule when we have early EIR field visits.
- Kids have started to notice I avoid freeway routes, even to Sacramento or Elk Grove.

4. Questions for Dr. Ruiz
- Am I ready to start very short freeway segments again if I’m not alone?
- How to distinguish normal nerves from actual panic attacks?
- Concrete exposure plan that won’t derail everything if I backslide.

5. To-do
- Check SacRT timetable for early bus to City Hall on days with 8:30 a.m. coordination calls.
- Ask Rosa if she’d be okay watching the kids one evening so Daniel can drive me to the next community meeting without rushing.
